summary: Two philosophy lecturers discuss Mary Midgley's views on the purpose of philosophy, drawing primarily from her book 'What is Philosophy For?'. Midgley argues that philosophy is a practical necessity rather than a private luxury, comparing philosophers to plumbers who must fix broken conceptual schemes and collaborate with creative thinkers to build coherent world pictures.

principles.statement: Philosophy is a necessity, not a private luxury.
principles.rationale: A coherent world picture is required to navigate life, and it is the philosopher's job to organize what concerns everybody rather than just looking inward.
principles.source_quote: that coherent world picture is not a private luxury it's something we all need for our lives

principles.statement: Philosophy is partly a creative discipline that requires collaboration with artists.
principles.rationale: To replace bad conceptual visions that cause chaos and to weave our lives back together, philosophers need the help of poets and other creative people.

mental_models.name: Philosophers as Plumbers
mental_models.description: Conceptual schemes are like elaborate plumbing systems. We don't notice them when they work, but when they break, philosophers must do the hard work of 'pulling up the floorboards' to find the blockage, disconnect the broken parts, and install new visions.
mental_models.example: When a conceptual scheme is no longer working properly and causing chaos, a philosopher acts like a plumber to sniff out the blockage and disconnect it.

signature_quotes.text: the philosopher's business is not as some people mistakenly think merely to look inward it is to organize what concerns everybody
signature_quotes.context: Describing the true job of a philosopher.

signature_quotes.text: philosophy aims to bring together those aspects of life that have not yet been properly connected so as to make a more coherent more workable world picture
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the goal of philosophical inquiry.

signature_quotes.text: i suspect that philosophical reasoning will now become rather important... we shall need to think about how best to think about new and difficult topics how to imagine them how to visualize them how to fit them into convincing world picture and if we don't do that for ourselves it's hard to see who will be able to do it for us
signature_quotes.context: The closing lines of her book 'What is Philosophy For?', written in 2018.

anti_patterns.description: Treating philosophy as a private luxury or an exercise in merely looking inward.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It fails to address the real-world chaos caused by broken conceptual schemes and neglects the philosopher's duty to organize what concerns everybody into a workable world picture.
